aliases: Fried Millet with Chicken and Garlic

english: Fried Millet with Chicken and Garlic

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for millet-chicken stir-fry. FOODON_00002473 (cooked grain dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Millet, cooked) used as closest parent; no composite millet-chicken dish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite millet-chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of garlic fried millet with chicken is approximately 350 g, comprising a base of cooked millet topped with stir-fried chicken pieces and charred garlic.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Millet, cooked (FDC ID 169702) and Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stir-fried (FDC ID 171477), combined in approximate 55:45 millet-to-chicken ratio with garlic and oil adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service – FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for kê gà cháy tỏi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrients estimated by combining USDA FDC values for cooked millet (~55% by weight) and stir-fried chicken meat (~40% by weight) with a small allowance (~5%) for garlic and cooking oil. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate recalculated proportionally. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of millet-based foods: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Millet (foxtail/pearl), cooked, GI ~54 (glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care – American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 derived from Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables for cooked millet (pearl/foxtail, glucose reference). The composite dish (millet + chicken + garlic) will have a lower effective GI due to protein and fat content; GI 54 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 55.3) / 100 ≈ 29.8, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 15.8 × 3.5 = 55.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from millet carbohydrate base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The millet base contributes approximately 55 g of carbohydrate per serving with a GI of ~54 (low-medium boundary), yielding a glycemic load of ~30; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and beneficial whole-grain millet
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is relatively low at approximately 3.2 g per serving, and millet provides dietary fibre and magnesium that support cardiovascular health; however, sodium from soy sauce should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kê gà cháy tỏi (Garlic Fried Millet with Chicken) is a Vietnamese-origin dish served in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant scene, notably at establishments such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The dish features cooked millet (kê) as the grain base — an ancient whole grain with a mild, slightly nutty flavour and soft, slightly chewy texture — topped or mixed with stir-fried chicken pieces (gà) that have been cooked with generous amounts of garlic (tỏi) until the garlic is charred and fragrant (cháy). The cooking process involves high-heat wok or pan frying, which imparts a smoky, caramelised garlic aroma and a lightly crispy exterior to the chicken. Millet is nutritionally notable for its moderate glycemic index (~54), dietary fibre, magnesium, and B-vitamins, making it a more nutritionally diverse alternative to white rice. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, salt, and pepper, contributing meaningful sodium levels (~320 mg per 100 g). Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to soy sauce sodium, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the millet carbohydrate load (~55 g per serving, GL ~30), and low concern for cardiovascular disease given its modest saturated fat content. It is a protein-rich, whole-grain-based mixed dish suitable as a main course. Typical restaurant serving size is approximately 350 g and is priced around SGD 20 in Singapore.
common_user_queries: What is kê gà cháy tỏi?
Calories in garlic fried millet with chicken
Is kê gà cháy tỏi good for diabetics?
Glycemic index of millet chicken dish

confidence_reason: No exact entry for kê gà cháy tỏi exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FDC entries for cooked millet and stir-fried chicken in approximate proportions. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed international GI tables for cooked millet. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC source entries used for this estimation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC source entries used for this estimation. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the combined source estimation. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in stir-fried chicken and cooked millet per USDA FDC data. 5. The food name 'Kê gà cháy tỏi' is Vietnamese; the cuisine classification is Singapore because the dish is served in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant context (GrabFood Singapore source). Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) with Vietnamese also included as the dish's language of origin. 6. GI of 54 is a conservative upper-bound estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 for cooked millet; the composite dish GI is likely lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. 7. Sodium estimate of 320 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce seasoning adjustment above the plain millet/chicken baseline.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Established canonical English food name from Vietnamese original 'Kê gà cháy tỏi'. Applied Singaporean multilingual naming rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as dish origin language.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is trace/confirmed negligible per USDA FDC. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (exact, closest_parent, none). Confirmed GI is numeric >0 with peer-reviewed source.
